beats.text: Here's a three-by-three matrix — nine entries arranged in three rows and three columns.

Every term in the determinant picks exactly one entry per row, one per column — no repeats. Arrows trace three paths through the matrix simultaneously, each grabbing one entry from each row and column.
Six paths total — that's three-factorial. Each path multiplies its three entries together. Even permutations get a plus sign, odd ones get a minus. The determinant is their sum.
For a two-by-two: just two paths. One grabs a then d — plus sign. The other grabs b then c — minus sign. Result: a-d minus b-c.

For anything bigger, cofactor expansion breaks the problem down. A highlight crosses out row one and column one, leaving a two-by-two. Its determinant is the cofactor. Repeat across the row — nesting smaller and smaller until every piece is a two-by-two base case.
Signed products, recursive structure — that's the whole engine behind every determinant.
